reference_materials: Equations and Constants sheet (includes all key formulas for thermodynamics, kinetics, equilibrium, acids/bases, electrochemistry, gases, and atomic structure)
exam_overview.calculator_policy: Scientific or graphing calculator allowed for entire exam (both sections)
tips: You receive a full formula and constants sheet — do not waste study time memorizing equations; invest it in understanding when and why to use each one
Always balance chemical equations before doing any stoichiometry or equilibrium calculation
Particulate diagrams (submicroscopic representations) appear frequently — practice drawing and interpreting them at the atomic/molecular level
Free-response questions reward mechanistic reasoning: explain why, not just what
Use units throughout every calculation and cancel them explicitly — a unit mismatch is a fast way to catch an error

units.name: Atomic Structure and Properties
key_topics: Empirical and molecular formulas from percent composition
Electron configuration (Aufbau, Hund's rule, Pauli exclusion)
Periodic trends: atomic radius, ionization energy, electronegativity, electron affinity
Valence electrons and ionic compound formation
key_concepts: Coulomb's law explains periodic trends: greater nuclear charge or smaller distance means stronger attraction, which raises ionization energy and decreases radius
Core electrons shield valence electrons from the full nuclear charge; more shielding reduces effective nuclear charge felt by outer electrons
A PES spectrum peak position reflects binding energy (closeness to nucleus); peak height reflects relative number of electrons in that subshell
Average atomic mass is a weighted average — elements with one dominant isotope have an atomic mass close to that isotope's mass
Electron configuration drives chemical reactivity: elements in the same group have the same valence electron count and similar chemistry
learning_objectives: 1.1.A: Calculate moles from mass using n = m/M and Avogadro's number
1.2.A: Interpret mass spectra to determine isotope masses, abundances, and average atomic mass
1.5.A: Write ground-state electron configurations using the Aufbau principle and explain electron-nucleus interactions via Coulomb's law
1.6.A: Relate a PES spectrum to electron configuration and explain peak positions using binding energy
1.7.A: Predict and explain trends in ionization energy, atomic/ionic radius, electronegativity, and electron affinity using effective nuclear charge and shielding
common_question_types: Match a PES spectrum to an element or ion by counting peaks and comparing relative heights
Calculate average atomic mass from isotope abundances given in a table
Predict which element in a group or period has the highest ionization energy or smallest radius and justify using Coulomb's law
Determine empirical formula from percent composition data
exam_tips: On PES questions, the highest binding energy peak is always the innermost subshell (e.g., 1s); count peaks to find the number of occupied subshells and match to an element
Exceptions to Aufbau (e.g., Cr, Cu) are NOT assessed — don't worry about them
When asked why ionization energy increases across a period, invoke effective nuclear charge (Z_eff), not just 'nuclear charge' alone

units.name: Compound Structure and Properties
key_topics: Bond types: ionic, polar covalent, nonpolar covalent, metallic
Potential energy vs. internuclear distance (bond length and bond energy)
Lewis diagrams including formal charge and resonance
key_concepts: Bond type follows electronegativity difference: large difference means ionic, moderate means polar covalent, near-zero means nonpolar covalent; metallic bonding is a delocalized 'sea of electrons'
Formal charge = (valence electrons) - (lone pair electrons) - (1/2 bonding electrons); minimizing formal charge selects the best Lewis structure
Resonance structures are not real alternating structures but a quantum superposition; actual bond lengths and energies are intermediate between resonance extremes
VSEPR counts all electron domains (bonding + lone pairs) to predict geometry; lone pairs compress bond angles more than bonding pairs
Hybridization is determined by the number of electron domains on the central atom: 2 = sp, 3 = sp2, 4 = sp3; pi bonds form only from unhybridized p-orbital overlap
learning_objectives: 2.1.A: Classify bonds as ionic, polar covalent, or nonpolar covalent based on electronegativity differences and explain metallic bonding
2.5.A/2.6.A: Construct Lewis diagrams and apply formal charge and resonance to select the most representative structure
2.7.A: Predict molecular geometry, bond angles, and hybridization using VSEPR; distinguish sigma from pi bonds
common_question_types: Draw or evaluate a Lewis structure and identify formal charges or resonance contributors
Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles of a molecule using VSEPR
Identify the hybridization of the central atom and whether sigma or pi bonds are present
Compare bond lengths or bond energies for molecules with different bond orders
exam_tips: d-orbital hybridization (sp3d, sp3d2) and molecular orbital theory are NOT assessed
When choosing among Lewis structures, prefer the one with the lowest magnitude formal charges and negative formal charge on the more electronegative atom
Always count lone pairs when applying VSEPR — a tetrahedral electron geometry with one lone pair gives a trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry

units.name: Properties of Substances and Mixtures
key_topics: Intermolecular forces: London dispersion, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding, ion-dipole
Solid types: ionic, metallic, molecular, covalent network
Ideal gas law and Dalton's law of partial pressures
Kinetic Molecular Theory (KMT) and Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
key_concepts: Strength of IMFs determines boiling point, viscosity, and solubility: hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > London dispersion; London dispersion strength grows with molecular size and surface area
Ideal gas law (PV = nRT) assumes no intermolecular attractions and negligible particle volume; real gases deviate most at high pressure and low temperature when these assumptions fail
Temperature is proportional to average kinetic energy of gas particles, not speed; heavier molecules move slower at the same temperature
Solubility follows polarity: polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes; nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes
Beer-Lambert law (A = εbc) means absorbance is directly proportional to concentration — a calibration curve allows unknown concentration determination
learning_objectives: 3.1.A: Rank IMF strength from molecular structure and use IMF differences to explain physical property trends
3.4.A/3.5.A: Apply ideal gas law and Dalton's law; interpret Maxwell-Boltzmann distributions
3.6.A: Explain when and why real gases deviate from ideal behavior
3.10.A: Predict solubility using IMF principles ('like dissolves like')
3.13.A: Apply Beer-Lambert law to relate absorbance to concentration
common_question_types: Rank compounds by boiling point or melting point using IMF strength
Predict which solid type (ionic, molecular, covalent network, metallic) a substance forms and explain its macroscopic properties
Apply PV = nRT or Dalton's law to solve gas problems
Interpret or sketch a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution comparing two temperatures or two gases
exam_tips: Unit 3 is the largest unit by exam weight (18-22%) — expect 10-13 MCQs from this content alone
Colligative properties, molality, phase diagrams, and percent by mass/volume are NOT assessed

For Beer-Lambert questions, a higher molar absorptivity (ε) means the solution absorbs more strongly at that wavelength — deeper color does not always mean higher ε without knowing the path length and concentration

units.name: Chemical Reactions
key_topics: Evidence for chemical vs. physical changes
Balanced molecular, complete ionic, and net ionic equations
Precipitation, acid-base, and redox reaction classification
Oxidation numbers and balancing redox half-reactions
key_concepts: Net ionic equations show only the species that actually change; spectator ions (those that appear unchanged on both sides) are omitted
The limiting reagent determines the theoretical yield; use mole ratios from the balanced equation to find which reactant runs out first
Brønsted-Lowry acids donate H+ (protons); bases accept H+; every acid-base reaction produces a conjugate pair
In redox reactions, oxidation (loss of electrons) and reduction (gain of electrons) always occur together; assign oxidation numbers to track electron transfer
All Na+, K+, NH4+, and NO3- salts are soluble — a key solubility rule for predicting precipitate formation
learning_objectives: 4.2.A: Write balanced molecular, complete ionic, and net ionic equations for reactions in aqueous solution
4.5.A: Perform stoichiometric calculations including limiting reagent and percent yield
4.7.A: Classify reactions as precipitation, acid-base, or redox; identify combustion products
4.8.A: Identify Brønsted-Lowry acids/bases and conjugate pairs in a reaction
4.9.A: Assign oxidation numbers and write balanced redox half-reactions
common_question_types: Write the net ionic equation for a given reaction and identify spectator ions
Determine the limiting reagent and calculate theoretical yield from reactant masses
Identify a reaction as precipitation, acid-base, or redox and justify the classification
Identify the Brønsted-Lowry acid, base, conjugate acid, and conjugate base in a reaction
exam_tips: Lewis acid-base theory and the terms 'oxidizing agent'/'reducing agent' are NOT assessed in this unit
Memorize the short solubility rules: all Na+/K+/NH4+/NO3- are soluble; most Cl-/Br-/I- are soluble except with Ag+/Pb2+/Hg2 2+; most SO4 2- are soluble except Ba2+/Pb2+
In free-response questions, always write the net ionic equation unless specifically asked for the molecular equation

units.name: Kinetics
key_topics: Factors affecting reaction rate (concentration, temperature, surface area, catalysts)
Rate law and reaction orders from initial rate data
Integrated rate laws: zero, first, and second order
Reaction mechanisms: elementary steps and intermediates
Rate-determining step and mechanism-derived rate laws
key_concepts: Rate law orders are determined experimentally from data, never from stoichiometric coefficients in the balanced equation
The rate-determining step (slowest elementary step) controls the overall rate law; the rate law is written from the elementary stoichiometry of that step
Activation energy is the energy barrier that colliding molecules must overcome; a catalyst lowers Ea, increasing rate without changing the overall thermodynamics (no change in ΔH or K)
For first-order reactions, half-life is constant (independent of concentration) and is related to k by t1/2 = 0.693/k; radioactive decay is always first-order
A higher temperature shifts the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution so a greater fraction of molecules have energy above Ea, dramatically increasing the rate
learning_objectives: 5.2.A: Write and interpret rate laws; determine reaction order from initial rate data
5.3.A: Use integrated rate laws and graphical methods (ln[A] vs t, 1/[A] vs t) to determine order and calculate concentration or time
5.5.A: Explain successful collision requirements: sufficient energy and correct orientation
5.8.A: Derive the rate law from a proposed mechanism using the rate-determining step
5.11.A: Explain how catalysts increase reaction rate and why they do not alter the equilibrium constant
common_question_types: Determine reaction order and rate law from a table of initial rate data
Identify reaction order from the linear graph (which plot of [A], ln[A], or 1/[A] vs. time is a straight line)
Predict how changing temperature, concentration, or adding a catalyst affects the rate and rate constant
Given a proposed mechanism, identify intermediates, catalysts, and the rate law consistent with the rate-determining step
exam_tips: Arrhenius equation calculations (finding Ea from two rate constants) may appear but are not the primary focus — prioritize conceptual understanding of Ea and temperature effects
Intermediates appear as products of one elementary step and reactants of a later step; they do not appear in the overall reaction
The pre-equilibrium approximation applies when the first step is fast and reversible — substitute the intermediate concentration using the K expression for that equilibrium

units.name: Thermochemistry
key_topics: Specific heat capacity and calorimetry (q = mcΔT)
key_concepts: Enthalpy (ΔH) is a state function — it depends only on initial and final states, not the pathway; this underpins Hess's Law
Bond breaking always requires energy (endothermic); bond forming always releases energy (exothermic); net ΔH for a reaction is the difference
During a phase change, temperature is constant (energy goes into or comes from the phase transition, not molecular kinetic energy)
Calorimetry measures heat transfer using q = mcΔT; the system and surroundings exchange heat until thermal equilibrium is reached
Hess's Law: reactions can be added algebraically; reversing a step flips the sign of ΔH; multiplying a step scales ΔH proportionally
learning_objectives: 6.4.A: Apply q = mcΔT to calorimetry problems; apply the first law of thermodynamics
6.7.A: Calculate ΔH from bond energies using ΔH = Σ(bonds broken) − Σ(bonds formed)
6.8.A: Calculate ΔH°rxn from standard enthalpies of formation
6.9.A/B: Apply Hess's Law to calculate ΔH for a target reaction by combining given reactions
common_question_types: Use q = mcΔT in a calorimetry context to find heat of reaction or final temperature
Apply Hess's Law to combine two or three reactions (with reversals and scaling) to find ΔH for a target reaction
Calculate ΔH from bond energies and explain whether the reaction is endothermic or exothermic
Interpret an energy diagram: identify the activation energy, enthalpy of reaction, and transition state
exam_tips: The distinction between enthalpy and internal energy (ΔH vs. ΔU) and the concept of state functions are NOT assessed at a technical level — focus on application
Bond enthalpy calculations are approximate (average bond energies); formation enthalpy calculations using tabulated ΔHf° values are more accurate — both methods are assessed
When applying Hess's Law, write out each manipulation step explicitly: if you reverse a reaction, flip the sign; if you multiply, scale ΔH by the same factor

units.name: Equilibrium
key_topics: Writing Kc and Kp expressions (excluding solids and pure liquids)
Reaction quotient Q and comparison to K
ICE tables for equilibrium concentration calculations
Magnitude of K and product/reactant favorability
Le Châtelier's Principle: concentration, temperature, and pressure stresses
Solubility product Ksp and molar solubility
key_concepts: Equilibrium is dynamic: forward and reverse rates are equal, but both reactions are still occurring; concentrations are constant, not zero
Q vs. K determines direction: if Q < K the reaction proceeds forward (toward products); if Q > K it shifts backward; if Q = K the system is at equilibrium
Only temperature changes alter the value of K; changes in concentration, pressure, or volume shift Q and cause the system to re-establish equilibrium, but K is unchanged

Le Châtelier's Principle: a system at equilibrium shifts to partially counteract any imposed stress; adding reactant shifts toward products; increasing pressure on a gas equilibrium shifts toward fewer moles of gas
The common-ion effect reduces solubility: adding an ion that is already a component of the Ksp equilibrium shifts the equilibrium toward the solid, decreasing how much dissolves
learning_objectives: 7.3.A: Write Kc and Kp expressions; exclude pure solids and liquids; calculate Q and compare to K
7.7.A: Use ICE tables to calculate equilibrium concentrations from initial conditions and K
7.9.A: Apply Le Châtelier's Principle to predict the direction of shift for concentration, temperature, pressure, and dilution stresses
7.11.A: Calculate molar solubility from Ksp; explain how Ksp relates to solubility
7.12.A: Explain and calculate the common-ion effect on solubility
common_question_types: Write the equilibrium expression for a reaction and calculate K or Q from given concentrations
Use an ICE table to find equilibrium concentrations given initial concentrations and K
Predict the direction of shift in response to a Le Châtelier stress and explain using Q vs. K
Calculate molar solubility from Ksp or predict how a common ion affects solubility
exam_tips: Kc ↔ Kp interconversion is NOT assessed — you will not need to convert between them using Δn
For ICE tables with small K values, the 'x is small' approximation (ignoring x when added to a large initial concentration) is often valid — but always check if x/initial > 5%
Temperature is the only stress that changes the actual value of K; all other stresses just shift Q until it returns to K

units.name: Acids and Bases
key_topics: pH and pOH calculations; Kw at 25°C
Strong acid and strong base dissociation
Weak acid/base equilibria and Ka/Kb expressions
Buffers: composition, function, and Henderson-Hasselbalch equation
Acid-base titration curves and equivalence points
key_concepts: Strong acids and bases dissociate completely; for strong acids, [H3O+] = initial acid concentration directly; do not write Ka for strong acids
Weak acid equilibrium involves partial dissociation: Ka measures the extent; smaller Ka means weaker acid; Ka × Kb = Kw for a conjugate pair
A buffer resists pH change because it contains both a weak acid (neutralizes added base) and its conjugate base (neutralizes added acid) in large, roughly equal amounts

The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation (pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA])) shows that pH = pKa when the conjugate acid and base are in equal concentrations — this is the half-equivalence point on a titration curve
Acid strength depends on molecular structure: stronger electronegative atoms or more resonance stabilization of the conjugate base make a stronger acid
learning_objectives: 8.1.A/8.2.A: Calculate pH and pOH for strong acids and bases; apply Kw
8.3.A: Set up Ka and Kb ICE calculations; calculate percent ionization
8.8.A/8.9.A: Explain buffer action and apply Henderson-Hasselbalch to calculate buffer pH
8.5.A: Interpret titration curves; identify equivalence and half-equivalence points
8.6.A: Relate molecular structure to acid strength using electronegativity and conjugate base stability
common_question_types: Calculate pH of a weak acid or weak base solution using Ka or Kb and an ICE table
Apply Henderson-Hasselbalch to find the pH of a buffer or the ratio of conjugate pair needed for a target pH
Interpret a titration curve: identify the equivalence point, buffer region, and pKa from the half-equivalence point
Compare acid strengths of two compounds based on molecular structure and explain using conjugate base stability
exam_tips: Lewis acid-base theory is NOT assessed in AP Chemistry
At the half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration, pH = pKa exactly — this is an important landmark on the titration curve
Buffer capacity is greatest when pH ≈ pKa and when concentrations of both components are high; a buffer with equal concentrations of acid and conjugate base has maximum capacity at that pH

units.name: Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry
key_topics: Entropy: sign and magnitude of ΔS changes
Gibbs free energy (ΔG = ΔH − TΔS) and thermodynamic favorability
Relationship between ΔG° and K (ΔG° = −RT ln K)
Standard cell potential and standard reduction potentials
key_concepts: ΔG < 0 means thermodynamically favored (spontaneous); ΔG > 0 means not favored; ΔG = 0 means equilibrium — this does NOT tell you how fast the reaction goes
ΔG° = −RT ln K: if ΔG° < 0 then K > 1 (products favored); if ΔG° > 0 then K < 1 (reactants favored); a reaction can be thermodynamically favored but kinetically slow (high Ea)
In a galvanic cell, chemical energy converts spontaneously to electrical energy (E°cell > 0); in an electrolytic cell, external electrical energy drives a non-spontaneous reaction
Oxidation always occurs at the anode; reduction always occurs at the cathode — this holds for both galvanic and electrolytic cells
Entropy increases when: solids dissolve, gases form, the number of moles of gas increases, or temperature increases; entropy decreases when particles become more ordered
learning_objectives: 9.1.A/9.2.A: Predict the sign of ΔS from physical and chemical changes; calculate ΔS°rxn
9.3.A: Calculate ΔG° from ΔH° and ΔS°; predict thermodynamic favorability from signs of ΔH and ΔS
9.5.A: Use ΔG° = −RT ln K to relate free energy to the equilibrium constant
9.8.A: Distinguish galvanic and electrolytic cells; identify anode, cathode, and direction of electron flow
9.11.A: Apply Faraday's law (I = q/t, F = 96,485 C/mol e−) to calculate moles of electrons, mass deposited, current, or time in electrolysis
common_question_types: Predict the sign of ΔG from given ΔH and ΔS values and determine whether the reaction is favored at high or low temperature
Relate ΔG° to K: determine whether K > 1 or K < 1 from ΔG°, or vice versa
Describe a galvanic or electrolytic cell: identify the anode, cathode, direction of electron flow, and role of the salt bridge
Use Faraday's law to calculate mass deposited during electrolysis given current and time
exam_tips: Nernst equation algorithmic calculation is NOT the primary focus — understand qualitatively that as a galvanic cell operates Q increases, E decreases toward 0, and the cell reaches equilibrium when E = 0
Labeling electrodes as positive or negative is NOT assessed — focus on anode = oxidation and cathode = reduction
For ΔG = ΔH − TΔS problems, the temperature-dependence is key: if ΔH < 0 and ΔS > 0, always favored; if ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0, never favored; mixed signs depend on temperature magnitude
